feature_name: Dual operation modes: Root Complex and End Point
description: The controller supports both Root Complex (RC) and End Point (EP) roles, selectable via bootstrap pins or software. In RC mode it manages downstream configuration and filtering; in EP mode it exposes BARs and responds to upstream configuration. Mode can be changed by software after boot for flexibility. LTSSM is enabled by software to start link training in either mode. This enables a single IP to be used in host or device roles across platforms.
evidence: 1.3 Features; 1.4.1 PCIe Core Module; CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN]; DEVSTAT PCIESSMODE (ref. 1.4.1)
dependencies: Power domain on; PLL locked; Correct bootstrap or DEVSTAT writes
Remarks: Select mode before enabling LTSSM; reinitialize link when switching modes.

feature_name: PCIe link width and speed: x1/x2 at 2.5 or 5.0 GT/s
description: A single bidirectional link supports up to two lanes with Gen1 (2.5 GT/s) and Gen2 (5.0 GT/s) rates. Dynamic width conversion is supported for flexible lane usage. Automatic lane reversal and RX polarity inversion improve routing options. Negotiated speed and width are reported in Link registers. These capabilities enable scalable bandwidth with minimal board constraints.
evidence: 1.3 Features; LINK_CAP[MAX_LINK_WIDTH, MAX_LINK_SPEED]; LINK_STAT_CTRL[NEGOTIATED_LINK_WD, LINK_SPEED]; 1.3 (automatic lane reversal, polarity inversion)
dependencies: Common clock configuration for optimal training; Proper SerDes config; PLL lock
Remarks: Use LINK_CTRL2[TGT_SPEED] and PL_GEN2[DIR_SPD] to control speed changes post L0.

feature_name: 128-bit VBUSM master and slave interfaces
description: The module exposes one 128-bit VBUSM master port and one 128-bit VBUSM slave port to the SoC interconnect. The master port sources inbound transactions to system memory, while the slave port accepts outbound traffic and local MMR accesses. Both ports operate in RC and EP modes. This separation enables concurrent inbound and outbound data paths. It also simplifies DMA attachment on the slave side.
dependencies: SoC interconnect clock present; Address mapping configured
Remarks: Map slave side ranges for outbound data and MMR; ensure master port priorities via PRIORITY register.

feature_name: Address Space 0 mapping: application/local/remote/IO windows
description: A dedicated 16 KB Address Space 0 is partitioned into 4 KB regions for application registers, local configuration, remote configuration, and an RC-only remote IO window. Each region is accessed as non-burst 32-bit transactions. The mapping is consistent whether accessed locally or via PCIe (BAR0). This structure simplifies control, enumeration, and test. It also isolates IO from data paths.
dependencies: BAR0 mapped; APP registers accessible; LTSSM up for remote access
Remarks: Mark these windows as non-cacheable and avoid bursts; gate access on link-up state.

feature_name: Outbound address translation: 32 regions of 1/2/4/8 MB
description: Outbound transactions can be mapped through 32 equal-sized regions to PCIe address space. Region size is programmable (1–8 MB) and indexes are derived from internal address bits. Each region has low and high offset registers supporting 32/64-bit target addresses. This allows partitioning host memory space to multiple remote windows. It is essential for DMA-friendly flat mappings.
evidence: 2.7.1 Outbound Address Translation; OB_SIZE; OB_OFFSET_INDEXn; OB_OFFSETn_HI; Example 2-1
dependencies: CMD_STATUS[OB_XLT_EN]=1; Correct OB_SIZE and offsets; DMA uses mapped ranges
Remarks: Avoid TLPs that cross translation region boundaries; split at region edges if needed.

feature_name: Inbound address translation with BAR matching (4 regions)
description: Inbound memory TLPs are accepted against BARs and optionally remapped through four inbound regions. Each region specifies BAR association, start address, and internal offset, allowing scatter mapping of PCIe ranges into device memory. BAR0 is reserved to application registers and always targets Address Space 0. BAR1 can optionally serve as the inbound base by leaving IB_STARTn at zero.
evidence: 2.7.2 Inbound Address Translation; IB_BARn/IB_STARTn/IB_OFFSETn; 2.7.2.2 BAR0 Exception; 2.7.2.3 Using BAR1 Value
dependencies: BAR decoding programmed; CMD_STATUS[IB_XLT_EN]=1; Proper BAR size/masks
Remarks: Use multiple regions to map non-contiguous memory; prioritize highest matching start address per Table 2-6.

feature_name: Programmable BAR Mask registers for EP/RC
description: BAR Mask registers overlay BARs to define requested size/type and to enable/disable BARs before enumeration. Software must set CMD_STATUS[DBI_CS2]=1 to write masks, then clear after programming. Reads return BAR values, not masks; verify by test writes and masking behavior. BAR0 mask is constrained since BAR0 targets application registers. This mechanism allows precise memory aperture negotiation with the RC.
evidence: 2.7.3.1 BAR Mask Registers; CMD_STATUS[DBI_CS2]; 3.3.x/3.4.x BAR*_MASK tables
dependencies: DBI_CS2 gating; Enumeration sequence timing
Remarks: Do not modify mask registers from the serial link; program before RC enumeration.

feature_name: External DMA support for PCIe data transfers
description: The PCIe SS lacks an internal DMA, but supports high-throughput transfers when paired with system EDMA or similar engines. In RC mode, DMA can move data to/from remote EP memory spaces discovered during enumeration. In EP mode, inbound TLPs reach the master port directly for memory operations. Practical throughput is about 85% of 2.0 Gbps (Gen1) or 4.0 Gbps (Gen2) per lane each direction. Example EDMA setups illustrate payload-sized bursts for efficiency.
dependencies: Outbound address translation configured; Adequate interconnect bandwidth
Remarks: Size aCnt/bCnt to max payload boundaries; ensure non-cacheable mappings for PCIe spaces.

feature_name: MSI interrupt generation and reception (32 vectors)
description: The controller supports MSI generation from EP to RC and reception at both RC and EP sides. MSI address/data are configured in MSI capability and MSI_IRQ register space, with eight groups of four vectors mirrored into status/enable sets. Writes by EP to RC's BAR0/MSI_IRQ with a vector value assert the corresponding MSI. Software clears statuses and writes IRQ_EOI to complete events.
dependencies: MSI_CAP[MSI_EN]=1; MSI address/data programmed; BAR0 accessible
Remarks: Observe ordering hazards of posted writes; clear MSIn_IRQ_STATUS and write IRQ_EOI per event.

feature_name: Legacy INTx support (EP generate, RC receive)
description: Legacy interrupts are generated in EP via EP_IRQ_SET/CLR and received by RC with per-line status. RC aggregates INTA–D and software services endpoints via configuration space. INTx disable and EOI semantics are provided, with caveats on deassert message race versus EOI. This maintains compatibility with legacy PCI devices and stacks. Use only one of MSI or INTx per function.
dependencies: INT_PIN configured; Link operational; Interrupt mapping to CPU enabled
Remarks: Read downstream INTx status before EOI to avoid retrigger due to deassert races.

feature_name: Advanced Error Reporting (AER) with logging and masks
description: AER registers implement detailed uncorrectable/correctable error tracking with per-bit masks and severity selection. Header Log registers capture the first failing TLP header, aiding root-cause analysis. Root ports receive aggregated error messages and can generate system error notifications. First Error Pointer marks the earliest uncorrectable error latched. Software can enable/disable reporting per class and choose fatal/non-fatal handling.
dependencies: AER enabled in DEV_STAT_CTRL and ROOT_CTRL_CAP; Interrupts routed
Remarks: Disable ECRC if link partner lacks support; consult PCIE_ACCR[ECRC_*] notes.

feature_name: Device power states (D0–D3) and ASPM L0s/L1
description: The controller supports PCIe device power states D0, D1/D2 (optional), and D3hot/cold. Link power management includes ASPM L0s/L1 and software-driven transitions, while L2 is not supported. Relationship between device D-states and link states is defined to guide entry/exit. Power domain control requires PSC sequencing and LTSSM re-init after power cycles. PME generation and acknowledgment are available for system coordination.
evidence: 2.15 Power Management; 2.15.3 Table 2-11; 2.13.3 Power Domain Considerations; PMCAP/PM_CTL_STAT
dependencies: PSC power domain control; PMCAP/PM_CTL_STAT configured; Link idle for ASPM
Remarks: Honor non-support of L2; use PMRST_* and PMCMD for turnoff/PME messaging.

feature_name: Endian support with programmable byte swapping
description: Although the PCIe core is little-endian at pins, the subsystem supports big-endian systems using programmable byte-swizzle modes. Separate inbound and outbound rules swap on 1/2/4/8-byte boundaries per ENDIAN[ENDIAN_MODE]. Byte enables are swapped consistently with data. This allows seamless integration in big-endian SoCs without software data marshaling. Modes are effective only in big-endian device configuration.
dependencies: Device configured for big-endian; Correct ENDIAN_MODE set
Remarks: Use mode C (4-byte) for 32-bit MMRs; verify DMA buffers' alignment against swap granularity.

feature_name: Transaction rules: payload, alignment, bursts, and byte-strobes
description: Maximum internal transaction size is 128 bytes, and misaligned starts reduce max size to 120 bytes. Only incremental bursts are supported; fixed or wrap bursts can deadlock the bus. Write byte enables must form one contiguous run of ones without holes. These constraints ensure TLP framing and bridge behavior remain correct. Software and DMA must respect these boundaries to avoid unspecified behavior.
evidence: 2.10.1 Requirements (Address Alignment, Burst Type, Byte Strobe)
dependencies: DMA engine configuration; Buffer alignment to 8/128B boundaries
Remarks: Split large or misaligned transfers; ensure contiguous byte enables from first to last byte.

feature_name: PIPE and PHY loopback for test
description: Two loopback methods are provided for validation: PIPE (link-layer) loopback between a master and slave, and PHY loopback that feeds TX into RX internally. PHY loopback enables TLP loopback without a link partner when used in RC mode. Configuration involves SerDes loopback bits and forcing LTSSM states via PL_FORCE_LINK. Proper sequencing avoids sequence number mismatches and misrouted packets during test.
dependencies: RC mode for PHY loopback; Set loopback before training; Disable conflicting BAR/range filters
Remarks: Do not use configuration transactions in PHY loopback; ensure BAR0/limits do not capture looped TLPs.

feature_name: SerDes PLL configuration and lock monitoring
description: The integrated SerDes requires programming of reference clock multiplication and loop bandwidth before link enable. ENPLL starts the PLL; LOCK indicates stable operation within specified cycles. MPY selects the multiply ratio (commonly 25× for 100 MHz ref to 2.5 GHz line), and VRANGE/ENDIVCLK provide VCO/divider control. Status bits expose Rx/TX test failures, LOS detect, and sync indications per lane. Correct PLL bring-up is a prerequisite for any PCIe traffic.
evidence: 2.3 SerDes Configuration; PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL[ENPLL, MPY, LB]; PCIE_SERDES_STS[LOCK, LOSDTCTx]
dependencies: Stable REFCLK to PCIECLKP/N; Kicker unlock for chip-level regs; PSC enables power
Remarks: Wait >1 µs after ENPLL before LOCK sampling; choose LB per clock quality (Table 2-2).

feature_name: Link control, training, and speed change controls
description: Software controls LTSSM enable, common-clock advertisement, retrain, and directed speed change. COMMON_CLK_CFG must reflect shared reference-clock topology. LINK_CTRL2[TGT_SPEED] and PL_GEN2[DIR_SPD] transition the link from Gen1 to Gen2 after initial L0. Port Force Link allows entering specific LTSSM states during validation. These controls provide deterministic link bring-up and performance tuning.
evidence: LINK_STAT_CTRL[COMMON_CLK_CFG, RETRAIN_LINK]; LINK_CTRL2[TGT_SPEED, ENTR_COMPL, SEL_DEEMPH]; PL_GEN2[DIR_SPD]; PL_LINK_CTRL[DLL_EN]
dependencies: PLL locked; Partner supports Gen2 for 5.0 GT/s; Proper training sequences (NUM_FTS)
Remarks: When no common clock, expect longer training; set ACK/NFTS per ACK_FREQ for stability.

feature_name: Comprehensive interrupt mapping and EOI handling
description: Fourteen interrupt sources cover legacy lines, MSI groups, error classes, power/reset events, and link/reset indications. Each group has raw/enabled status and enable-set/clear registers. End-of-interrupt is acknowledged by writing the event number to IRQ_EOI. This structured mapping simplifies ISR demux and allows fine-grained masking. It supports both RC and EP contexts appropriately.
dependencies: Interrupt controller routing; Enable sets configured; Link operational for message-based IRQs
Remarks: Group MSI vectors by quartet to CPU lines; always clear status then write IRQ_EOI.

feature_name: Reset mechanisms: POR, system, and software hot reset
description: The PCIe SS supports power-on reset (cold), system reset (hard/soft), and software-initiated downstream hot reset. RSTCMD[INIT_RST] triggers a hot reset sequence downstream. Soft reset excludes sticky MMR bits and certain PM logic. After any reset sequence the software must wait at least 100 ms before issuing transactions, and up to 1.5 s timeout for device readiness. Proper reset sequencing avoids link stalls and preserves state expectations.
evidence: 2.13 Reset Considerations; 3.1.7 RSTCMD[INIT_RST]; 2.13.2 Note on 100 ms wait
dependencies: PSC sequencing if power-cycling; Quiesce traffic (FLUSH_N=0) before warm reset
Remarks: Check ACT_STATUS to ensure buffers are empty; retrain LTSSM after reset.

feature_name: Compliance and implementation references
description: The design complies with PCI Express Base Specification Revision 2.0 and integrates Synopsys DWC PCIe Dual Core v3.51a with TI SerDes 1.1.03. This ensures interoperability with Gen1/Gen2 ecosystems. Adherence to industry standards simplifies certification and driver reuse. It defines capability register layouts and semantics per spec. These references serve as a baseline for software assumptions.
dependencies: Standards-compliant partner devices; Correct capability exposure
Remarks: Keep capability IDs/pointers accurate in config space for OS enumeration.

feature_name: ECRC generation and checking controls
description: End-to-end CRC can be generated and checked under software control to harden data paths. PCIE_ACCR exposes capability and enables for both generation and checking. First Error Pointer indicates which uncorrectable error bit first asserted when multiple errors occur. When peers lack ECRC support, the feature should be disabled to prevent false errors. This provides a tunable integrity mechanism across heterogeneous platforms.
dependencies: Peer capability discovery; AER and MSI configured for error notification
Remarks: Disable ECRC in RC if endpoints do not advertise support; log first error pointer for diagnostics.

feature_name: Link bandwidth management and status notification
description: Root ports can generate Link Bandwidth Management and Autonomous Bandwidth Change notifications. Status bits indicate retrain-complete or autonomous changes without DL_Down. Optional interrupts are provided for RC. These features enable software to react to topology or reliability-driven width/speed changes. EPs treat these fields as reserved.
dependencies: RC role; Interrupt routing if used
Remarks: Use with care during recovery events; EP should ignore reserved bits.

feature_name: ACK/NAK frequency and power-aware timing tuning
description: ACK_FREQ and related fields program the ACKing cadence and training sequence counts. Separate latencies for L0s/L1 entries and common-clock NFTS selection allow stability/latency tradeoffs. Disabling ACKs or DLLPs is also possible for debug via LANE_SKEW. These settings help converge challenging links and reduce power. Defaults meet spec but can be adapted to board conditions.
dependencies: Stable link partner; Common clock knowledge; PHY margins
Remarks: Only adjust after validation; document deviations from default spec values.
